The Heartland Courier

COMM 467  ·  News Editing  ·  Headline Writing Exercise — Class Demo
How to submit: Fill in your name and all six text fields below — that's two headlines, two dek headlines and two captions. When finished, click Submit Work — you'll see a summary of your headlines and captions and their character counts. Then click Confirm & Submit, which will open your instructor's Google Form with your answers pre-filled. Just add your name and e-mail address there and hit Submit. Your instructor will receive your work automatically.

Instructions

For each story, write a main headline, a dek headline and a caption in the fields below. The color ruler above each field updates live as you type — keep your headlines and captions in the green zone.

  1. Main hed (32–40 chars): Active voice, present tense, no articles (a/an/the) unless essential, no period.
  2. Dek (50–65 chars): A concise sentence giving context. May use articles. No period.
  3. Caption (50–80 chars): A complete sentence, with a period, that places the image into the context of the story.
Too short
Acceptable range
Too long
Filled & in range ✓
Story One — Business
Main Headline 32–40 characters
— ✦ —
Dek Headline 50–65 characters

After 30 years of selling books on the corner of Main and Depot streets, Heartland Books will close its doors for the last time on Dec. 31. Owner Ruth Kaplan, 68, said she is retiring and was unable to find a buyer willing to keep the store running as an independent bookshop.

"I've had offers," Kaplan said, "but they all wanted to turn it into something else — a wine bar, a gift shop. I couldn't do that to this place." Kaplan opened the store in 1994 with a $40,000 loan from her parents and a collection of 2,000 used paperbacks. At its peak, the store employed six people and hosted more than 100 author readings a year.

City Councilwoman Patricia Dunn called the closing a blow to downtown. "Heartland Books was an anchor," she said. "When a place like that goes, it changes the character of a neighborhood."

Developer Marco Stellini confirmed he has been in talks with the building's owner about the space. He declined to say what he had in mind but said he expected to make an announcement "early in the new year."

Kaplan said she plans to sell her remaining inventory at a discount through December. Any books left over will be donated to the Heartland Public Library.

Story Two — Education
Main Headline 32–40 characters
— ✦ —
Dek Headline 50–65 characters

The Heartland Unified School District is proposing to eliminate its high school marching program at the end of the academic year, citing a $1.4 million budget shortfall that administrators say cannot be closed without cutting electives.

Superintendent Carol Baines said the decision was among the hardest she had faced in 14 years leading the district. "These kids are extraordinary," she said. "But so are reading specialists, school counselors and functioning boilers. We have to make choices."

The Jefferson High School Marching Program — a fixture at the Heartland Fourth of July parade and the city's fall festival for more than two decades — would cease to exist under the proposal. The program has roughly 120 members and is directed by Frank Delgado, who has taught at Jefferson for 22 years.

"This band has marched in every Fourth of July parade since 1998," Delgado said. "These are kids who don't always thrive in a traditional classroom. For a lot of them, this is the reason they come to school."

A group of parents calling itself Save Heartland Music has begun circulating a petition and plans to speak at the school board's next meeting on Thursday. The group says it has already collected more than 900 signatures.

The board is expected to vote on the proposal at its January meeting. If approved, the cuts would take effect at the start of the next school year.
